name: Case Study - Jacki Rosin ARPS | RPS Distinctions Panel

step.name: Why did you want to gain a distinction with the Royal Photographic Society (RPS)?
step.text: To be honest, I had not thought about doing a distinction, but once Alan had suggested the idea I thought that having such a goal to reach would help me develop my photography

step.name: Describe how you found the RPS Distinctions Mentoring Classes.
step.text: The classes were very helpful in that not only did you have your own work looked at in detail but you were also able to see how others were progressing. Everyone collaborated ideas and suggestions, so there was a lot of support and feedback, not just from Alan but from others on the same journey

step.name: What was your experience like of the RPS Assessment Day?
step.text: I attended an RPS advisory day about halfway into the course and my panel changed quite a lot as a result. I then applied for online advice when I felt that I was almost ready to be assessed. Again a few tweaks but it helped to consolidate my panel

step.name: Did you seek independent advice, feedback or input from anywhere else before your panel assessment?
step.text: I attended an advisory day before my assessment, which I found really useful. I had my prints professionally printed and mounted and sought their advice on suitable papers and mounts to use .

step.name: Having completed the course and Distinction Assessment, what advice would you like to share with others?
step.text: You have to be prepared to put in a lot of time and effort. Sometimes it’s difficult to keep the enthusiasm going, especially when images are rejected, but eventually, you will see your panel beginning to come together

step.name: What was the hardest part of gaining a distinction with the RPS and why?
step.text: Producing 15 images that were varied enough for the judges, but that also worked cohesively to produce a panel
